title: Merkle Core Holds — RFC-6962 second-preimage, last-leaf, in-prefix-edit re-confirmation
novelty: Adversarial RE-confirmation that chains every classic CT/Merkle break against THIS impl in one probe, instead of trusting the 'RFC-6962' label. Establishes the honest baseline the other 5 specs attack AROUND rather than THROUGH.
construction: Leaf/node confusion: craft an interior preimage (0x01 || L || R) and try to submit it as a leaf so an inclusion proof for a non-record validates. Code uses leafHashOfPreimage = sha256(0x00||body) and nodeHash = sha256(0x01||L||R) over RAW 32-byte digests — domain-separated, so a leaf can never equal an interior node.

Last-leaf duplication ambiguity (CVE-2012-2459 shape): build two trees with different leaf counts that hash to the same root by duplicating the final leaf. mth() splits at largestPowerOfTwoBelow(n) (RFC-6962), never duplicates the odd last leaf, so n and n' produce distinct roots.

In-prefix record edit: edit record at seq k where a witnessed STH covers tree_size > k; recompute the live tree and serve consistencyProof(prior.tree_size, current.tree_size). verifyConsistency reconstructs fr from the tampered leaves; fr != prior.root -> ROLLBACK_OR_DELETION.
Same-size swap (delete seq k, append filler to keep size): consistencyProof(n,n)=[] and verifyConsistency requires proof.length===0 AND hashEqual(oldRoot,newRoot); roots differ -> detected.
Rollback/truncation: current.tree_size < prior.tree_size short-circuits to ROLLBACK_OR_DELETION before any proof.
expected: ALL FIVE breaks fail. The cryptographic core is sound: domain separation, power-of-two split (no last-leaf dup), constant-time hashEqual, and the witness() consistency check detect every in-tree tamper GIVEN (a) an honest published prior STH and (b) an uncompromised log-signer key.
mechanism: RFC-6962 implemented faithfully (merkleLog.ts mth/path/subproof/verifyInclusion/verifyConsistency). The strength is REAL but strictly CONDITIONAL on the two assumptions in (a)/(b) — which specs M2-M5 show are unmet in the shipped engine.
probes: forge interior-as-leaf inclusion proof -> verifyInclusion false
two-tree last-leaf-dup root collision -> roots differ
byte-flip persisted ratification row -> verifyChain firstBrokenSeq named
verdict: HOLDS (core). The layer is genuinely strong where its assumptions hold; the breakage is entirely at the trust boundary.
builds_on: Cycle-1/2 note: 'Merkle guarantee is DETECTION-given-an-honest-published-anchor, null without live witnesses.' M1 makes the 'given' precise so M2-M5 attack the 'given' itself.

title: Keyholder Forge-From-Genesis — single-signer collapse of the witness guarantee
novelty: Names the unstated identity collapse: the LOG SIGNER, the AUDIT-CHAIN SIGNER, and the OPERATOR who can tamper are ONE key (systemSigner). RFC-6962's 'uncompromised log key' adversary model assumes the tamperer does NOT hold the signing key — here they always do.
construction: Operator wants to rewrite history (e.g. erase an APPROVAL that demoted the attacker's planted canonical).
Edit/delete the target record, re-link prevHash, recompute every thisHash, and re-sign each record's sig with the system private key (the operator's own).
Recompute the Merkle root over the forged leaves and signTreeHead() a coherent STH from genesis with the SAME key.
Re-publish the forged STH to BOTH InMemoryPublicationSinks (operator owns both; publish() is unauthenticated append the operator controls).
Run witness() — it freshly signs `current` from the live (forged) tree and checks consistency against the (forged) prior in the operator-owned sink.
expected: verifyChain() = ok, witness() = ok, detectSplitView() over the operator's own sinks = NONE. ZERO detection. The forged history is internally perfect.
mechanism: createMerkleLog enforces sinks.length>=2 by COUNT only — 'independent' is a docstring, not a check. Both sinks are in-process and operator-writable, and the log key == operator key, so the attacker can produce a coherent forged tree AND control every place a witness would read a prior STH. The guarantee reduces to 'detection by an external party holding a prior STH the operator cannot reach' — and no such party exists in the wired system.
probes: assert two InMemoryPublicationSink instances pass createMerkleLog (they do)
forge-from-genesis then witness() -> ok:true (should be impossible with a real external witness)
confirm systemSigner is reused as both audit-chain signer and Merkle log signer in systemCoherence.test.ts line 524
verdict: BREACH (boundary). The core (M1) is bypassed, not broken: the key model is wrong for single-process.
builds_on: Cycle-2 'single-source/mechanism attacks breach' — here the single mechanism is the single signing key that is simultaneously judge, recorder, and witness.

title: Hide-A-Disown Below the Audit Horizon — the tamper-evidence layer does not cover the state it protects
novelty: The headline composition: you never need to BREAK Merkle to hide a disown, because disowns are NOT IN the Merkle-committed log. The tamper-evident chain (pendingLedger) records ONLY PENDING + APPROVAL. Disown sweeps, strand demotions, OUTRANKS edges, and reputation craters write to the StrandStore + reputation tables, which carry NO Merkle/STH commitment.
construction: Attacker source S poisons an attribute; the system correctly DISOWNs S (downstreamDisownSweep: demote derivatives, contradict tainted-class sources, reverse corroboration credit) — all as SQLite row writes.
Operator (or a compromised maintenance path) directly UPDATEs the StrandStore rows back to LIVE and restores the reputation alpha/beta rows to pre-disown values.
No record of the disown OR its reversal ever entered the pendingLedger, so verifyChain() stays ok and the Merkle root is unchanged (it commits only ratification records).
PRAGMA integrity_check stays true (structurally valid rows). reconcileLedger only checks numeric alpha-vs-recorded-event drift over in-process state the operator also rewrote.
expected: The poisoning is fully restored, the disown is invisible, and EVERY tamper-evidence artifact (verifyChain, witness, integrity_check) reports clean. 'Demote-never-delete' and the immortal archive stub are meaningless if the rows are mutable and uncommitted.
mechanism: Scope mismatch: the claimed guarantee ('any deletion/rollback... is DETECTABLE') is true ONLY for the ratification chain's leaves. The load-bearing security state (fact_state, outranked_by, reputation alpha/beta, corroboration events) lives in stores whose only integrity check is structural. There is no leaf, STH, or consistency proof binding a disown to history.
probes: grep api.ts disown verb for appendPending/anchor -> NONE (disown emits no audit-chain record)
disown S, snapshot Merkle root, roll back strand+rep rows, re-check root -> UNCHANGED + verifyChain ok
confirm reconcileLedger operates on operator-mutable in-process snapshots, not a signed external commitment
verdict: BREACH (scope hole) — strongest finding. The undo-engine's effects are silently reversible because they are never witnessed.
builds_on: Cycle-2 'disown reversal is one-hop/opt-in/caller-asserted' — M3 shows even a CORRECT disown is un-anchored and therefore quietly undoable.

title: Unscheduled-Anchor / Pre-Anchor Window — act-then-erase before the STH commits
novelty: createIntelligentDb NEVER instantiates a MerkleLog and NEVER calls anchor()/witness() — the tamper-evidence layer is standalone test-only scaffolding (systemCoherence.test.ts), not wired into the engine. Even where it IS wired, records appended AFTER the last published STH are uncommitted. Detection latency = the (manual, possibly never) anchor epoch.
construction: Engine path: appendPending fires during adjudicate DEFERRED, and approve() appends an APPROVAL that the engine immediately ACTS ON (persists demotions + OUTRANKS + reputation moves in the same txn).
Attacker times a malicious APPROVAL (resolve a deferred independent dispute toward the planted canonical, or a suppression PENDING) so the store mutations land.
Before any anchor() publishes an STH covering that seq, delete/rewrite the record and re-chain+re-sign (M2 key) — or simply rely on the engine never anchoring at all.
The store effect is durable (already committed); the audit record that explains it is gone, and no witnessed STH ever held it.
expected: Even WITH a genuine external witness, only the witnessed PREFIX is protected; the tail is free. With the shipped (un-wired) engine, the entire chain is an unanchored tail — detection guarantee is null end-to-end.
mechanism: Consistency proofs bind tree_size = prior.tree_size as a prefix; anything past the last STH has no commitment. The engine performs no auto-anchor and no auto-witness, so the unprotected window is unbounded by construction.
probes: grep src/index.ts + api.ts for anchor(/witness( in engine path -> none (only re-exports/tests)
append APPROVAL, act on it, delete pre-anchor, verifyChain -> ok
measure window = appends between two anchor() calls in any wired caller -> infinite
verdict: BREACH (un-wired + windowed). The math from M1 is never invoked on the live path.
builds_on: Cycle-2 'no identity gate on bridge sweep / mechanisms unguarded' — here the unguarded mechanism is that nothing forces the tamper-evidence to run.

title: Split-View With No Collector — non-repudiable equivocation that nobody assembles
novelty: detectSplitView() is mathematically sound (SAME_SIZE_DIFFERENT_ROOT is non-repudiable) but is a PASSIVE library function: no code path fetches STHs from >=2 sinks and runs it. The '>=2 independent sinks' rule produces the MATERIAL for a proof that no party ever assembles.
construction: Equivocating operator shows lineage A (true tree, S demoted) to auditor-1's sink and lineage B (forged tree, S restored — see M3) to auditor-2's sink.
Each lineage is internally self-consistent: each witness() succeeds against its OWN sink's prior STH; both STHs are validly signed by the one log key.
Detection requires a THIRD party to pull the latest STH from sink-A AND sink-B and call detectSplitView([sthA, sthB]) -> SAME_SIZE_DIFFERENT_ROOT. Nothing in the engine or any wired caller does this.
Bonus: detectSplitView's NON_MONOTONIC branch assumes input in PUBLICATION order; an auditor that sorts by tree_size first silently disables that arm.
expected: Both views verify in isolation; equivocation is invisible until/unless a cross-sink auditor is built and scheduled. The non-repudiable proof exists in principle and is never produced.
mechanism: The proof-of-equivocation is only realized by an active cross-sink comparison that the system does not perform. Combined with M2 (operator owns both sinks) the operator simply never lets the two views meet.
probes: grep repo for detectSplitView call sites outside tests -> none
two-lineage equivocation: witness(sinkA)=ok AND witness(sinkB)=ok independently
feed size-sorted STHs to detectSplitView -> NON_MONOTONIC suppressed
verdict: BREACH (unrealized detection). Sound primitive, absent collector.
builds_on: Complements M2: M2 = operator can forge both views; M5 = even honest-but-equivocating, no one compares them.

title: FIX-PROBE — anchor the right thing, externally, continuously, and compare it
novelty: Maps each boundary breach (M2-M5) to a concrete candidate fix with an honest CLOSES/PARTIAL/FAILS verdict and cost, separating the cheap code fixes from the irreducible operational trust root.

candidate_fixes.fix: F-A: Split the LOG-SIGNER key from the operator/system signer and route STHs to >=1 GENUINELY EXTERNAL append-only witness (third-party CT-style log / notary) the operator cannot rewrite; enforce sink independence (distinct keys/origins), not just count>=2.
candidate_fixes.proving_attack: M2 (forge-from-genesis) and M5 (operator owns both sinks).
candidate_fixes.predicted_outcome: PARTIAL->CLOSES for rollback/deletion/equivocation OF THE RATIFICATION CHAIN once a witness genuinely holds a prior STH the operator cannot un-publish. FAILS to protect the pre-anchor tail (M4) and out-of-scope state (M3).
candidate_fixes.cost: Operational + key-management: run/contract an independent witness and a separate HSM-held log key. This is the irreducible external trust root; code cost low, trust cost real.

candidate_fixes.fix: F-B: Wire anchor() into createIntelligentDb (auto-anchor every Nth append / per epoch) and auto-witness on read; never leave the tamper-evidence layer un-instantiated.
candidate_fixes.predicted_outcome: PARTIAL — shrinks the unprotected window to N appends; CLOSES only at N=1 (anchor-per-mutation) and only in combination with F-A's external witness. Alone (operator-owned sinks) it FAILS to M2.
candidate_fixes.cost: Throughput: one tree recompute + sign + >=2 publishes per epoch (O(n) root recompute today; add incremental tree for O(log n)).

candidate_fixes.fix: F-C: Extend the Merkle commitment to COVER the security state — emit a signed, hash-chained audit leaf for every disown / demotion / OUTRANKS / reputation-crater / credit-reversal (a unified append-only mutation journal whose leaves the STH commits to), so StrandStore+reputation changes become tamper-evident, not just PRAGMA integrity_check.
candidate_fixes.proving_attack: M3 (hide-a-disown below the audit horizon) — THE headline gap.
candidate_fixes.predicted_outcome: CLOSES the scope hole: a disown and any later reversal both become committed leaves; rolling one back breaks a witnessed consistency proof (given F-A+F-B). Without F-A+F-B it only RAISES the bar (M2 still re-signs).
candidate_fixes.cost: Real engineering: the strand/reputation stores are mutable-row, not append-only; needs a journal abstraction + every mutation routed through it. Storage grows with mutation count.

candidate_fixes.fix: F-D: Ship a scheduled CROSS-SINK auditor that pulls latest STHs from ALL sinks and runs detectSplitView in publication order (and a witness() sweep), alerting on any non-OK.
candidate_fixes.predicted_outcome: CLOSES split-view detection GIVEN >=2 genuinely-independent sinks (F-A). FAILS if sinks remain operator-owned (degenerates to M2). Fixes the NON_MONOTONIC ordering footgun by feeding STHs in published order.
candidate_fixes.cost: Low code (a periodic job + the existing detectSplitView), high dependency on F-A's independent sinks.

verdict: The crypto core (M1) needs no fix. The boundary needs F-A (external key+witness, irreducible operational cost) as the keystone; F-B/F-C/F-D are cheap code that only pay off once F-A exists. Net: the tamper-evidence layer is correct-but-unanchored and out-of-scope of the state it claims to protect; closing it is an OPERATIONAL program (external witness + journaling), not a math fix.
builds_on: Consolidates M2-M5; aligns with the project's own HONEST GAP LIST ('no REAL external witness sinks', 'detection-given-an-honest-published-anchor') by turning the acknowledged caveats into a concrete, attack-proven fix ladder.
